title_en: The Industry Map: Who Does What and Where the Money Pools
title_zh: 行业地图:谁做什么、钱在哪
tagline_en: After this module you can place any company in the AI supply chain on one map — and predict its gross margin, its capex burden, and its pricing power before you ever open its 10-K.
tagline_zh: 读完本讲，你能把 AI 产业链上的任何一家公司放进同一张地图——不用翻 10-K，就能大致猜出它的毛利率、资本开支负担和议价权。

sections.heading_en: One chip, a dozen hands: the silicon side of the map
sections.heading_zh: 一颗芯片，十二双手：地图的硅片端
sections.body_en: No company on Earth makes an AI chip alone. Think of publishing a book. Someone writes it, someone sells the word processor, someone licenses the fonts, a printing house prints it, machine makers built the presses, and a bindery assembles the final copies. The chip industry splits the same way, and each role has a name. EDA (electronic design automation) — Cadence, Synopsys, Siemens EDA — sells the design software: you cannot hand-draw 200 billion transistors, so every chip on the planet is designed inside these tools. IP vendors — Arm is the archetype — license pre-designed circuit blocks (a CPU core, a memory interface) and collect a royalty on every chip shipped. Fabless companies — Nvidia, AMD, Qualcomm, Broadcom — write the 'book': they design chips, build the software ecosystem, and own the customer, but operate no factories. Foundries — TSMC, Samsung Foundry, Intel Foundry — are the printing houses: contract manufacturers who turn designs into physical silicon; TSMC alone holds over 90% of the world's advanced-node capacity. IDMs (integrated device manufacturers) do both design and manufacturing in-house — Intel historically, and crucially the three memory makers (Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron), who mass-produce the HBM stacks every AI chip depends on. OSATs — ASE, Amkor — are the binderies: outsourced assembly, packaging, and test, historically the low-glamour end, though AI-era advanced packaging (Module 06's CoWoS) pulled the most valuable packaging work back into TSMC itself. Finally, equipment makers — ASML, Applied Materials, Lam Research, KLA, Tokyo Electron — build the presses: the lithography, deposition, etch, and inspection machines every fab must buy, with ASML holding a literal monopoly on EUV lithography.
sections.body_zh: 地球上没有任何一家公司能独立造出一颗 AI 芯片。拿出版一本书打比方：有人写书，有人卖文字处理软件，有人授权字体，印刷厂负责印刷，设备商造印刷机，装订厂完成成书。芯片行业就是按这套分工切开的，每个角色都有专名。EDA（电子设计自动化）——Cadence、Synopsys、西门子 EDA——卖的是设计软件：2000 亿个晶体管不可能靠手画，全世界每一颗芯片都诞生在这几家的工具里。IP 厂商——Arm 是原型——把预先设计好的电路模块（一个 CPU 核、一个内存接口）授权出去，每出货一颗芯片就抽一笔版税。无厂设计公司（fabless）——英伟达、AMD、高通、博通——是「写书的人」：他们设计芯片、构建软件生态、握住客户，但名下没有一座工厂。晶圆代工厂（foundry）——台积电、三星代工、英特尔代工——是印刷厂：受托把设计变成物理硅片，仅台积电一家就掌握全球 90% 以上的先进制程产能。IDM（垂直整合制造商）设计与制造一手包办——历史上的英特尔是代表，更关键的是三家存储厂（三星、SK 海力士、美光），所有 AI 芯片赖以生存的 HBM 堆栈都出自它们之手。OSAT（委外封测）——日月光、安靠——是装订厂：外包的组装、封装与测试，历来是产业链里最不起眼的一环，不过 AI 时代的先进封装（第 06 讲的 CoWoS）已经把最值钱的封装环节拉回了台积电自己手里。最后是设备商——ASML、应用材料、泛林、科磊、东京电子——他们造「印刷机」：光刻、沉积、刻蚀、检测，每座晶圆厂都绕不开，其中 ASML 对 EUV 光刻机是字面意义上的独家垄断。

sections.heading_en: From chip to token: the compute-economy layers
sections.heading_zh: 从芯片到 token：算力经济的那几层
sections.body_en: The map doesn't end when the chip is packaged — that's only where the AI-specific half begins. Hyperscalers — Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta — buy chips by the hundreds of thousands, build the datacenters, and sell the result as cloud computing. All four also design their own AI chips (Google's TPU, Amazon's Trainium, Microsoft's Maia, Meta's MTIA), which makes them part-time fabless companies and Nvidia's most dangerous customers. Neoclouds — CoreWeave, Nebius, and dozens of smaller firms — are specialist GPU landlords: they do one thing (rent out Nvidia GPUs by the hour) and their economics are pure real estate — borrow money, buy an asset, and earn a spread. An H100 costs a well-financed operator roughly $1.4–1.6 per GPU-hour all-in (capital, debt, power, datacenter) per SemiAnalysis TCO estimates, and rented for ~$2/hour at the best deals: a thin, leverage-sensitive margin, nothing like Nvidia's. AI labs — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, xAI — convert rented compute into models and sell tokens; they are the demand engine for everything upstream, and coding is the killer vertical — it drove Anthropic's API revenue up ~13x y/y, while OpenAI's mix still skews consumer chat (SemiAnalysis, Anthropic Growth and Bedrock Mix). A useful bridge metric across these layers: one megawatt of AI datacenter capacity generates roughly $10–12M of AI cloud revenue per year — which is why the industry increasingly quotes itself in megawatts and gigawatts rather than chips. When a lab signs a '1 GW deal,' you can now translate: about $10–12B a year of compute spending flowing up the chain you just mapped.
sections.body_zh: 芯片封装完成，地图并没有走完——AI 特有的那半张才刚刚开始。超大规模云厂商（hyperscaler）——微软、谷歌、亚马逊、Meta——几十万颗地买芯片、盖数据中心，再把结果打包成云计算卖出去。四家都还自研 AI 芯片（谷歌 TPU、亚马逊 Trainium、微软 Maia、Meta MTIA），等于兼职做了 fabless，也因此是英伟达最危险的客户。新兴云（neocloud）——CoreWeave、Nebius 和几十家更小的玩家——是专职的「GPU 包租公」：只做一件事（按小时出租英伟达 GPU），其商业模式是纯粹的地产逻辑——借钱、买资产、赚利差。一颗 H100 对资金成本良好的运营商而言全包成本约 $1.4–1.6/GPU·小时（本金、利息、电力、机房，据 SemiAnalysis TCO 估算），最好的行情能租到约 $2/小时：利差很薄、对杠杆极其敏感，和英伟达的生意完全不是一回事。AI 实验室——OpenAI、Anthropic、Google DeepMind、xAI——把租来的算力炼成模型、按 token 卖钱；它们是整条上游的需求引擎，而编程才是最杀手级的垂直场景——它把 Anthropic 的 API 收入同比推高了约 13 倍，而 OpenAI 的收入结构仍偏向消费级聊天（SemiAnalysis，Anthropic Growth and Bedrock Mix）。跨越这几层有一个好用的换算桥：1 兆瓦（MW）AI 数据中心容量，一年大约对应 $1,000–1,200 万的 AI 云收入——这就是为什么这个行业越来越用兆瓦、吉瓦而非芯片颗数来报价。下次看到某实验室签了「1 GW 大单」，你可以直接翻译：每年约 $100–120 亿的算力开支，将沿着你刚画好的这张地图逐层向上流。

sections.heading_en: The margin ladder: why each layer earns what it earns
sections.heading_zh: 毛利率阶梯：每一层凭什么赚那么多（或那么少）
sections.body_en: Line the layers up by gross margin — the share of each sales dollar left after direct production costs — and a striking ladder appears. EDA and IP sit at ~80–90%: software economics, a near-duopoly, and a price that is a rounding error next to the $100M+ chip project it enables. Nvidia earns ~75% on AI systems — it sells hardware at roughly 4x its build cost. TSMC printed 62.3% in 4Q25. Equipment makers cluster around 45–60% (KLA at the top). Memory swings wildly with the cycle — deeply negative in busts, above 50% in booms — because standard DRAM is a commodity; HBM is slowly making it less of one. OSATs scrape by at roughly 15–20%. The mechanism behind the ladder is not effort or even technology — it is scarcity times switching cost, divided by how visible your price is to the customer. EDA and ASML face almost no competition; Nvidia's CUDA software moat makes switching painful mid–AI race; TSMC's process lead means there is nowhere else to print a frontier chip. OSATs, by contrast, compete with each other on price for work anyone can do. One more axis matters: capital intensity. Nvidia's 75% needs no factories; TSMC's 62% must first pay for the most expensive factories ever built. High margin plus low capex (EDA, fabless) prints free cash flow; high margin plus brutal capex (TSMC, memory) is a treadmill where the margin is the reward for shouldering risk no one else will take.
sections.body_zh: 把各层按毛利率——每 1 美元销售额扣除直接生产成本后剩下的比例——排个队，会看到一道醒目的阶梯。EDA 和 IP 站在约 80–90%：软件经济学、接近双寡头的格局，而且其价格相对它所支撑的上亿美元芯片项目来说只是个零头。英伟达在 AI 系统上赚约 75%——硬件大约按成本的 4 倍卖。台积电 2025 年四季度毛利率 62.3%。设备商聚集在 45–60% 一带（科磊居顶）。存储则随周期剧烈摆动——萧条时深度亏损，景气时冲上 50% 以上——因为标准 DRAM 是大宗商品；HBM 正在慢慢改变这一点。OSAT 只能挣个 15–20% 的辛苦钱。这道阶梯背后的机制不是勤奋、甚至不是技术，而是「稀缺性 × 切换成本 ÷ 你的价格在客户账单里有多显眼」。EDA 和 ASML 几乎没有竞争对手；英伟达的 CUDA 软件护城河让客户在 AI 竞赛正酣时切换代价惨重；台积电的制程领先意味着前沿芯片没有第二个地方可印。反观 OSAT，干的是谁都能干的活，只能彼此杀价。还有一条轴同样重要：资本强度。英伟达的 75% 不需要一座工厂；台积电的 62% 得先垫付人类史上最贵的厂房。高毛利 + 低资本开支（EDA、fabless）是印钞机；高毛利 + 恐怖资本开支（台积电、存储）则是跑步机——那份毛利，是对扛下别人不敢扛的风险的补偿。

sections.heading_en: Fab economics: the most expensive factories ever built
sections.heading_zh: 晶圆厂经济学：人类史上最贵的工厂
sections.body_en: Foundry economics deserve their own stop on the map, because they gate everything else. The numbers are hard to absorb on first read. Intel says a leading-edge fab costs $25–30B of capex per 10,000 wafers per week of capacity; TSMC has cited about $42B per 10,000 wafers for its 3nm fabs in Arizona. TSMC's capital budget for 2026 alone is $52–56B — more than the annual capex of any Western oil major, and rivaling Saudi Aramco, the world's biggest oil spender (~$53B in 2024). A single EUV lithography tool costs ~$225M, and a fab needs dozens. Why does anyone sign up for this? Because each tool is a money press when busy: that $225M machine patterns more than $650M worth of wafers per year. The catch is the cost structure: a fab's costs are almost entirely fixed — depreciation (the giant purchase price expensed over ~5 years) plus staff and facilities that run 24/7 regardless of output. A fab resembles an airline: the marginal cost of filling one more seat (printing one more wafer) is small, and an empty slot is revenue lost forever. That makes utilization the single most important variable in foundry economics. In the 2023 downturn, TSMC's 7nm utilization fell from ~100% to below 60% within three quarters (≈83% in Q4'22 → <70% in Q1'23 → <60% in Q2'23), and margins sagged immediately. This is why foundries demand customer prepayments, why TSMC refuses to overbuild even when AI customers beg for wafers — underwriting a $40B fab against demand that might evaporate is how foundries die — and why the current AI boom keeps slamming into a silicon supply ceiling: capacity discipline learned in past busts.
sections.body_zh: 代工厂的经济学值得在地图上单独停一站，因为它卡着其余所有环节的脖子。这些数字第一次看很难消化：英特尔自己说，一座前沿晶圆厂每 1 万片/周的产能需要 $250–300 亿资本开支；台积电给过的口径是其亚利桑那 3nm 厂每 1 万片约 $420 亿。仅 2026 年一年，台积电的资本预算就是 $520–560 亿——直逼全球开支最大的沙特阿美（2024年约$530亿），超过任何一家西方石油巨头的年度 capex。一台 EUV 光刻机约 $2.25 亿，而一座厂要买几十台。图什么？因为机器一旦转起来就是印钞机：那台 $2.25 亿的设备一年能曝光超过 $6.5 亿产值的晶圆。代价在成本结构上：晶圆厂的成本几乎全是固定的——折旧（把天价采购在约 5 年里摊销）加上不论产量多少都 24 小时运转的人员与厂务。晶圆厂像一家航空公司：多印一片晶圆的边际成本很小，而一个空出来的机台时段就是永远消失的收入。于是产能利用率成了代工经济学里最重要的单一变量。2023 年下行周期里，台积电 7nm 利用率在三个季度内从约 100% 掉到 60% 以下（Q4'22 约 83% → Q1'23 低于 70% → Q2'23 低于 60%），毛利率应声下挫。这解释了为什么代工厂要收客户预付款；为什么哪怕 AI 客户跪求晶圆，台积电也拒绝大干快上——押 $400 亿建厂去赌一个可能蒸发的需求，正是历史上代工厂的死法；也解释了为什么这轮 AI 热潮一次次撞上硅片供给的天花板：那是历次萧条教会全行业的产能纪律。

sections.heading_en: Follow the AI capex dollar — and watch where the pool moves
sections.heading_zh: 跟着 AI 资本开支的一美元走——盯住钱池的移动
sections.body_en: Now run one dollar of hyperscaler AI capex through the map. Most of it buys compute systems, and here the flow gets lopsided: Nvidia sells at ~75% gross margin, so of every dollar a cloud pays for an Nvidia system, roughly 75 cents stays in Santa Clara as gross profit and only ~25 cents flows on to the people who physically made it. Of that manufacturing cost, HBM memory alone is about half — which is why the memory makers are the second-biggest AI winners — while TSMC's wafer and packaging take a smaller slice than most people guess. The markup applies to everything in the box: one GB200 rack carries ~$551k of optical transceivers at cost, which becomes ~$2.2M by the time the customer pays. The rest of the capex dollar buys networking, land, buildings, and increasingly power infrastructure. Then the dollar tries to come back: each megawatt deployed earns $10–12M a year of cloud revenue, out of which neoclouds keep a thin rental spread and labs keep whatever their token prices exceed compute costs. The key investing insight is that the pools move. In 2023–25 the money pooled at the bottlenecks: Nvidia, HBM, CoWoS packaging, then power equipment. By 2025–26, serving costs collapsed while token demand exploded, and value began pooling downstream in the model labs — Anthropic's inference gross margin swung from -94% in 2024 to 38% in 2025 to above 70% in 2026. The map is static; the money is not. Ask, every year: which layer is scarce now?
sections.body_zh: 现在，让超大规模云厂商的 1 美元 AI 资本开支在地图上走一遍。大头买了算力系统，而这里的流向极不均匀：英伟达按约 75% 毛利率卖货，云厂商付给英伟达系统的每 1 美元里，约 75 美分作为毛利留在了圣克拉拉，只有约 25 美分流向真正动手制造的人。而在这份制造成本里，仅 HBM 存储就占了大约一半——这就是存储厂成为 AI 第二大赢家的原因——台积电的晶圆加封装拿到的份额反而比多数人想象的小。加价覆盖箱子里的一切：一个 GB200 机架里成本约 $55.1 万的光模块，到客户手里变成约 $220 万。资本开支剩下的部分买了网络、土地、厂房，以及越来越多的电力基础设施。然后这 1 美元开始往回赚：每部署 1 MW，一年挣 $1,000–1,200 万云收入，其中新兴云只留下薄薄的租金利差，实验室则赚 token 售价超出算力成本的部分。对投资者最关键的洞察是：钱池会移动。2023–25 年，钱池积在瓶颈处：英伟达、HBM、CoWoS 封装，然后是电力设备。到 2025–26 年，推理服务成本崩塌、token 需求爆发，价值开始向下游的模型实验室汇集——Anthropic 的推理毛利率从 2024 年的 -94% 摆到 2025 年的 38%，再到 2026 年的 70% 以上。地图是静态的，钱不是。每年都要问一遍：今年，稀缺的是哪一层？

images.caption_en: TSMC's Fab 12A at its Hsinchu headquarters. The foundry layer owns the most expensive factories ever built — $25–42B of capex per 10,000 wafers of weekly leading-edge capacity — and because costs are almost entirely fixed, utilization is the single variable that makes or breaks its margins.
images.caption_zh: 台积电新竹总部的 Fab 12A。代工层拥有人类史上最贵的工厂——每 1 万片/周的前沿产能需要 $250–420 亿资本开支——而由于成本几乎全是固定的，产能利用率是决定其毛利率生死的唯一变量。

images.caption_en: Nvidia's Santa Clara headquarters (2005-era offices): offices, not factories. The fabless model — design the chip, own the software ecosystem, let TSMC do the manufacturing — is how a company with no fab keeps ~75% gross margin on AI systems while its manufacturing partners shoulder the capex.
images.caption_zh: 英伟达圣克拉拉总部（2005 年的办公楼）：只有办公楼，没有工厂。fabless 模式——自己设计芯片、掌握软件生态、把制造交给台积电——让一家没有晶圆厂的公司在 AI 系统上稳拿约 75% 的毛利，而资本开支由制造伙伴来扛。

own_fig.concept_en: The AI supply-chain map as one horizontal flow, with a gross-margin ladder and a capex-intensity strip, plus the '$1 of AI capex' flowing back through it
own_fig.concept_zh: 一张横向流动的 AI 产业链地图：上方毛利率阶梯、下方资本强度条，再叠加一条「1 美元 AI capex」的回流路径
own_fig.spec: Single wide panel, left-to-right flow of 8 boxes connected by arrows, each box = one layer with representative tickers underneath: [EDA/IP: Cadence, Synopsys, Arm] → [Fabless: NVDA, AMD, AVGO] → [Foundry: TSMC, Samsung, Intel Foundry] → [Memory IDM: SK Hynix, Samsung, Micron (HBM)] → [OSAT/Packaging: ASE, Amkor + TSMC CoWoS] → [Hyperscalers: MSFT, GOOG, AMZN, META] → [Neoclouds: CoreWeave, Nebius] → [AI Labs: OpenAI, Anthropic]. Equipment layer [ASML, AMAT, LAM, KLA, TEL] drawn as a box ABOVE the Foundry+Memory boxes with downward arrows labeled 'sells tools to fabs'. ABOVE the flow: a bar chart aligned to each box showing typical gross margin: EDA/IP ~80–90%, Fabless (NVDA AI systems) ~75% (source: SemiAnalysis GB200 BOM), Foundry 62.3% (TSMC 4Q25, SEC 6-K), Memory 'cyclical: <0% to >50%', Equipment ~45–60%, OSAT ~15–20%, Hyperscaler cloud ~30–40% op margin, Neocloud 'thin spread: ~$1.4–1.6/hr cost vs ~$2/hr rent (SemiAnalysis AI Cloud TCO model)', AI labs 'inference GM: -94%→38%→>70% (Anthropic 2024→2026, SemiAnalysis AI Value Capture)'. BELOW the flow: a capex-intensity strip (icons: $ to $$$$$): EDA/IP=$, Fabless=$, Foundry=$$$$$ ($52–56B TSMC 2026 budget; $25–42B per 10k wafers/wk), Memory=$$$$, OSAT=$$, Hyperscaler=$$$$$, Neocloud=$$$$ (debt-funded), Labs=$$$ (rented). A red dashed arrow runs RIGHT-TO-LEFT along the bottom labeled 'follow the $1 of AI capex': hyperscaler $1 → ~75¢ Nvidia gross profit + ~25¢ manufacturing cost → of which ~half = HBM (SemiAnalysis On Device AI), remainder = TSMC wafer + packaging + components. Right edge annotation: '1 MW deployed ≈ $10–12M/yr AI cloud revenue (SemiAnalysis Onsite Gas)'. Title the figure: 'Who does what — and who keeps the margin'.

why_en: This map is the router for the entire series: every sector brief that follows is a zoom-in on one box. For an investor it converts headlines into positions. A company's layer tells you its business model before you open the filings — gross margin, capex burden, cyclicality, and pricing power are properties of the box, not the logo. The margin ladder shows who keeps each AI capex dollar: Nvidia ~75 cents gross of its systems dollar, HBM half the chip's build cost, OSATs pennies. And the pools move with scarcity — GPUs, then HBM and packaging, then power, now model labs — so the habit is to ask which layer is this year's binding constraint, and own that toll booth.
why_zh: 这张地图是整个系列的路由器：后面每一篇行业简报，都是对图上某一个方框的放大。对投资者而言，它把新闻标题翻译成头寸。一家公司所在的「层」在你打开财报之前就告诉了你它的商业模式——毛利率、资本负担、周期性、议价权，这些是方框的属性，不是公司 logo 的属性。毛利率阶梯告诉你每 1 美元 AI 资本开支到底被谁留下（英伟达在其系统收入里留下约 75 美分毛利；HBM 占芯片制造成本一半；OSAT 只捡到硬币）。而钱池随稀缺性移动——先是 GPU，再是 HBM 与封装，然后是电力，如今轮到模型实验室——所以可复用的动作是：每年问一次「今年卡脖子的是哪一层」，然后去持有那一层的收费站。

extra_sources.source_name: TSMC 4Q25 Earnings Release (Jan 15, 2026), pp.1–2 (verified via Wayback snapshot 20260117073643)
